qa_pairs.question: What was the main purpose of the Advanced Research Computing infrastructure renewal?
qa_pairs.answer: The renewal aimed to improve High Performance Computing (HPC) and Cloud services for Canadian researchers by replacing nearly 80% of equipment that was approaching end-of-life, providing faster processing, greater storage capacity, and improved reliability.

qa_pairs.question: When did the infrastructure renewal begin, and when were new systems made available?
qa_pairs.answer: The renewal began in winter 2024–2025, and new systems were made available to users throughout 2025 and early 2026.

qa_pairs.question: Which new systems replaced the older ones as part of the infrastructure upgrade?
qa_pairs.answer: Arbutus replaced Cloud, Rorqual replaced Béluga, Fir replaced Cedar, Trillium replaced Niagara and Mist, and Nibi replaced Graham.

qa_pairs.question: What should researchers consider due to unavoidable outages during the installation and transition period?
qa_pairs.answer: Researchers should consider the possibility of outages when planning research programs and graduate examinations.

qa_pairs.question: Which users are currently able to access the upgraded Arbutus Cloud?
qa_pairs.answer: The upgraded Arbutus Cloud is now in production and available to RAC 2026–2027 recipients.

qa_pairs.question: What actions are required for users with 2025–2026 allocations on legacy Arbutus?
qa_pairs.answer: Both RAC recipients and RAS users with 2025–2026 allocations on legacy Arbutus are responsible for migrating existing resources before August 31, 2026.

qa_pairs.question: How do projects with existing Arbutus RAS Cloud allocations get access to the new system?
qa_pairs.answer: They must submit a request to cloud@tech.alliancecan.ca to be added to the provisioning queue.

qa_pairs.question: What new authentication method does the Arbutus Cloud use?
qa_pairs.answer: The new Arbutus Cloud introduces single sign-on (SSO) authentication with multi-factor authentication (MFA, requiring users to select “Authenticate using Digital Research Alliance of Canada”).

qa_pairs.question: What is the status of the Béluga compute service and its storage?
qa_pairs.answer: The Béluga compute service was stopped with the deployment of Rorqual and will not return to service. Storage quotas were reduced in January 2026, /scratch was decommissioned on February 28, 2026, and all remaining data access will end on June 20, 2026.

qa_pairs.question: Is the Béluga Cloud impacted by the decommissioning of the Béluga compute cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: No, Béluga Cloud (`beluga-cloud`) is a separate infrastructure and is not impacted by the decommissioning.

qa_pairs.question: When were the Niagara and Mist compute clusters retired, and what is the recommended next step for users?
qa_pairs.answer: Niagara was retired on September 30, 2025, and Mist on September 16, 2025. Users should transition new work to Trillium.

qa_pairs.question: What happened to files stored on the Cedar and Graham clusters after their retirement?
qa_pairs.answer: Files stored on Cedar are available on Fir, and files on Graham are available on Nibi, as these clusters share the same file systems. No action is required for stored files.

qa_pairs.question: How will the Resource Allocation Competition (RAC) be impacted by this transition?
qa_pairs.answer: The application process remains the same, but the availability of 2024/25 and 2025/26 allocations will change, with a possible period where neither award is available before new clusters are fully in service. Default allocations will be available on new clusters immediately.

qa_pairs.question: Where can users find more training opportunities related to the new infrastructure?
qa_pairs.answer: Users can discover more training opportunities through Explora (explora.alliancecan.ca).

qa_pairs.question: What kind of training is offered for new or prospective HPC users on Alliance Clusters?
qa_pairs.answer: Simon Fraser University (SFU) / West DRI offers a 3-part series on Introduction to HPC on Alliance Clusters, covering hardware, tools, software, compiling codes, using Slurm, benchmarking, and managing permissions.

qa_pairs.question: Is data automatically copied to new systems during the renewal activities?
qa_pairs.answer: No, data migration to the new systems is the responsibility of each National Host Site, who will inform users of necessary actions.

qa_pairs.question: Will my files be deleted during a complete data centre shutdown as part of renewal activities?
qa_pairs.answer: No, /project and /home data will be migrated by the National Host Site, and tape systems for backups and /nearline data remain unchanged.

qa_pairs.question: How are outages communicated to users?
qa_pairs.answer: Specific outages are described on the system status web page (status.alliancecan.ca), with general updates provided on the wiki page and via email.

qa_pairs.question: Whom should users contact for questions about the infrastructure transition?
qa_pairs.answer: Users should contact technical support for any questions regarding the transition.

qa_pairs.question: Will existing jobs and applications run on the new systems, and will current software be available?
qa_pairs.answer: Generally, yes, but new CPUs and GPUs may require recompilation or reconfiguration of some applications. The standard software environment and commercial, licensed software will be available.

qa_pairs.question: Can old hardware be purchased after equipment upgrades?
qa_pairs.answer: Most of the retired equipment is e-cycled by the host institution following their guidelines, rather than being sold. Users should contact the host institution directly regarding their specific policies.
